title: Martin Luquet tentative website
text:
I am a postdoc researcher in ecology. Currently, I work at the Laboratory for Alpine Ecology, where I study some behavioural aspects of predator-prey interactions.
This is my attempt to create my website using Quarto. I created this thanks to Cecilia Baldoni in a SORTEE workshop. Thank you Cecilia! It’s quite messy for now though haha.
I aim at using this as a place of expression. Not just as a resume or something. I don’t want to oversell myself or anything of that kind. I’m not really into the career stuff anyway, but I’d like to use this to express my doubts about the whole scientific research entreprise, my insecurities, also provide a list of my publications but pinpoint their limits. There may be a blog too!

title: Martin Luquet
text:
Ok here I’d like to do something a bit like Cecilia. With the list of publications as a tab. Also I’ll put my CV there I guess. But for now let’s do something less fancy.



My current project. How do wild mammals respond when extirpated predators are coming back? Well, maybe not that much. At least, not in most current European ecosystems, that are so shaped by humans. Focusing on two emblematic species: the chamois (one of our Alpine moutain “goats”) and wolves.



A chamois (Rupicapra rupicapra)



This is a chamois, by the way (Rupicapra rupicapra, note that it’s actually not a goat, contrary to the alpine ibex). This picture was taken by Tristan Mary, a colleague of mine: check his Instagram page, his pictures are cool.
I’m quite interested about methodological questions about these kind of topics: habitat selection, activity pattern analysis, etc. May write a bit about this one of these days.






Insect ‘pests’ are top-down regulated by other insects in agroecosystems - predators like ladybirds, and parasitoids. So in a way, they are our natural insecticides. That’s cool and all, but as you guess, it’s not that simple: we can’t just count of them to secure our food production. Is there a way to boost/stabilise the populations of these natural regulators? Maybe we can try to design our agroecosystems to provide them with the adequate resources. For instance, parasitoids need carbs as fuel. So can we bring them additional sugar sources to help them ‘perform’? Are they actually sugar-limited at all? Though it may seem like simple questions, it’s not. The literature is still not clear about this. The studies we carried out with my colleagues from France, Spain and Chile also provide mitigated results. But they also highlight that these are actually difficult questions to answer - as is often the case in ecology, really.

Below, you can find a parasitoid feeding: this is Aphidius ervi (a parasitoid of aphids) feeding on the nectary of a faba bean. This beautiful picture was taken by Artzai Jauregui. Yes, I am lucky enough to work with many talented photographers.




Aphidius ervi feeding on Vicia faba extrafloral nectary



This question occupied me a lot, during my PhD and after - although I try to put this aside because I have too much going on in my brain.
Also, I’m a bit fed up with all the productivity-focused rhetoric surrounding these issues (performance, etc.). I still get the strong impression that what’s needed is a change in the production model, not more research into ecological micro-mechanisms.
And frankly, I get the feeling that, deep down, we all know it. Mind you, I’m not saying: it’s important to secure food production; it’s easy to forget that when you’ve got enough to eat. I’m not saying that research is completely useless either. But I do sometimes get the impression that we often try to force our research questions (which are sometimes fundamental) to fit with agronomic issues (which are much more applied and not necessarily focused on ecological mechanisms, but rather on the effects on yield), in a way that’s often artificial and unsatisfactory. Don’t you think that produces rather rubbish stuff that’s not much use? Maybe I’ll try and write about that one of these days.






Two main things here:

Can we improve our practices so that our science is more open? To other scientists, but also to the people. Involved in SORTEE, mainly through collaborative projects, hopefully more in the future.
How reliable is our whole field of research? Can we improve it (through open science, or other practices)? Even if not, to me, it’s important to know what kind of ‘knowledge’ we are actually acquiring when doing ecological research - so that I can embrace all the limits when drawing conclusions. Curious about it, so I read a lot, but also working a little bit on if with SORTEE.

title: Martin Luquet
text:
I am a postdoc researcher working at the interface between behavioural and population ecology, mainly using quantitative methods. Currently, I work at the Laboratory for Alpine Ecology, where I study the spatial and temporal responses of chamois (a beautiful mountain ungulate) to wolves.
I do many other stuff though. I also worked a lot (and still do a little bit) on insects in agroecosystems. May seem quite messy, but always connected to the interface between behaviour, population ecology, and predator-prey (or host-parasitoid) relationships. Also taught some stats (not was I very good at it) and ecology. I’m very interested in Open Science and try to invest myself a little bit in it - not as much as I’d like to.
I’ve also always wondered a lot about the status of the discipline and what it enables us to learn and do (in fact, that’s where my interest in open science comes from). I’ve read a lot on the subject and racked my brains over it quite a bit. Not that meta-science and the philosophy of science are really ‘hobbies’—I just can’t help myself, haha. As a result, my thoughts on the subject are rather jumbled and not really organised methodically—nevertheless, perhaps I’ll share them here.

title: Martin Luquet
text:
21/05/2026. Just saw this citation by Scott Peacor today on Dynamic Ecology blog today.


I want my work to have a positive influence, not just have fun. This is not fun for me. I don’t really like statistics and I don’t like criticizing people. I don’t get a kick out of it. And so designing a statistic project to show that we ecologists are doing something wrong is not my idea of fun.



Found it striking (and inspirational)? Reminds me the famous citation from Richard McElreath: ‘Research is a job, not a hobby’ (famous at least for me, haha).
